dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for an unconscious male at a local business in [CITY].
Hello, my name is John, an EMT with the rescue squad. What led to the 911 call today?
A man, about 60 years old, was playing a video poker machine when he suddenly collapsed and is unresponsive.
Patient is unconscious.
No eye opening, verbal response, or motor movement observed at this time.
Airway is being managed by RFD with BVM and oxygen at 15L.
Patient is apneic with diminished bilateral breath sounds.
No wheezing or rales detected. Rhonchi are present bilaterally.
Carotid pulse is bounding and palpable.
Blood pressure is 162 over 92 as measured upon arrival.
Oxygen saturation is 98% with BVM ventilation in place.
Blood sugar is 125 mg/dl via finger stick.
EKG shows sinus rhythm on the cardiac monitor.
Can you share your name and where you are right now, sir?
He collapsed at a video poker machine. No known medical history.
He is 61 years old, male. No known allergies or medications.
Pupils are small and sluggishly reactive bilaterally.
Surgical scar is noted on the right wrist. No reported pain.
Abdomen is distended, non-tender, and not rigid.
Breathing appears adequate, with diminished bilateral breath sounds, normal lung expansion bilaterally noted upon re-exam.
Partner, draw up and administer 0.5mg naloxone via IV.
IV placed in the left antecubital vein. Secure and patent with aspiration of blood noted upon insertion.
Pulse is 69, BP is 137/84, with no spontaneous breathing detected.
Oxygen saturation is now 85% on room air after naloxone administration. Respiratory drive present with shallow breaths observed.
Glucose unchanged at 125 mg/dl. Not hypoglycemic per current readings.
Let's transition to a non-rebreather mask at 15L for improved oxygenation.
Respirations are 10 per minute but spontaneous and adequate with good effort observed.
SpO2 has improved slightly to 88% with the non-rebreather, though remains low and fluctuating. Respiratory drive is intact with consistent breathing effort observed.
EKG remains in sinus rhythm through 4-lead monitoring, no arrhythmias or ectopic beats observed.
Pt appears more alert. Let's give another 0.25mg naloxone.
Vitals: pulse 89, BP 148/76, respirations 10, SpO2 85%. GCS is now 14 post-naloxone dose.
Any memory of the event before collapse, sir?
No, I just feel tired.
Skin is clear; no bites, rashes, bruises, or abrasions noted. Warm and dry.
12-lead ECG was successfully obtained per protocol, no acute ST changes or abnormal rhythms noted.
Pt responded to naloxone. Following protocol for altered mental status.
Pt has become more somnolent. Let's give another 0.25mg naloxone.
Partner, prepare to administer 0.25mg naloxone via IV again.
Pulse is 73, BP is 117/70, respirations 16. GCS is 15, full eye opening and orientation in place.
Do you take any medications or use any drugs?
No, I don't take any.
Understood, no known drug or medical history to report.
Respirations stable at 16 with nasal cannula at 4L; satisfactory respiratory exchange observed.
We are transporting you to [COMPANY_NAME]. Is that acceptable?
Partner, secure the patient and console for transport. We are preparing to move.
Patient is secured and ready for transport. Console is locked in place.
Current pulse 77, BP 106/67, respirations 15. SpO2 90% via non-rebreather. Stable per current assessment.
Pt still denies any opioid or medication use and refuses further community resources or referrals. Proceeding to hospital as requested.
Patient is secured safely. Ready for transport from the scene.
Vitals: pulse 73, BP 117/70, respirations 16. SpO2 90%. No decline observed. Respiratory drive adequate throughout.
Pt's wife is expected to join him at the hospital. No further action needed per protocol.
EKG has remained in sinus rhythm throughout transport, stable and well-tolerated.
Capnography stays low in the 20s, consistent throughout episode.
Pt remains in denial about opioid use. Proceeding to hospital with care as refusal is noted with documentation.
Check the patient one last time and prepare for transport.
Final readings: pulse 73, BP 117/70, respirations 16. SpO2 90%. No further deterioration.
Pt is stable for transport. Final destination is confirmed as [COMPANY_NAME].
All transport equipment is secured, and we are en route now.
Glucose 0 on second check. No hypoglycemia. Will be rechecked at the hospital.
Pt refuses further discussion. Noting refusal in documentation.
Patient is safely transferred and the report is finalized. No unsecured belongings noted.
Pt scooted himself to the ED bed independently. No difficulty noted, no apparent injuries.
Capnography remains in the low 20s during transport, steady.
Pt is stable and final destination is confirmed. Transport is now complete with no transport-related incident.
Community resources and post-discharge care discussed with pt at roadside. Pt declined additional referral.
Verified scene is cleared and family has been coordinated. Call terminated upon transfer to ED.
Pt reported no pain through the entirety of the encounter.
